_doc: Real, publicly documented regulatory survey findings related to environmental cleaning, per facility type. Every entry carries a citation number and a source URL that was fetched. Nothing here is inferred from the text of a regulation — a finding is what an inspector actually writes up, and on a site selling compliance an invented one is the most expensive mistake available. Where a regulator publishes no citation frequency, `frequency` is null rather than estimated.

skilled-nursing.finding: No effective infection prevention and control program — surveyors cite what they observe directly: hand hygiene missed between residents, isolation precautions not implemented, shared resident-care equipment (glucometers, blood-pressure cuffs, lifts, shower chairs) not cleaned and disinfected between residents, soiled linen handled or stored improperly.
skilled-nursing.frequency: The most-cited F-tag nationally. Computed from the CMS Health Deficiencies file (June 2026 refresh): 7,377 of 13,841 facilities nationally (53.3%) and 152 of 296 Massachusetts facilities (51.4%) were cited on their most recent standard health survey.

skilled-nursing.finding: Food storage, preparation and service not sanitary — soiled can openers, ice machines and cooler gaskets, unclean prep surfaces, undated or uncovered food, dish-machine sanitiser at the wrong concentration.
skilled-nursing.citation: F812 — 42 CFR 483.60(i), Food Procurement, Store, Prepare, Serve Sanitary
skilled-nursing.frequency: Second most-cited nationally: 6,341 of 13,841 facilities (45.8%) and 98 of 296 in Massachusetts (33.1%). Usually cited at wide scope, meaning the surveyor found it affected most or many residents.

skilled-nursing.finding: Resident's right to a safe, clean, comfortable and homelike environment not honoured — soiled floors and furnishings, odours in resident rooms, stained or torn privacy curtains and mattresses.
skilled-nursing.citation: F584 — 42 CFR 483.10(i), Safe, Clean, Comfortable, Homelike Environment
skilled-nursing.frequency: 2,346 of 13,841 facilities nationally (16.9%) and 51 of 296 in Massachusetts (17.2%).

skilled-nursing.finding: Facility environment not maintained safe, functional and sanitary — soiled utility and housekeeping rooms, unsanitary tub and shower rooms, damaged or unsealed surfaces that can no longer be properly cleaned.
skilled-nursing.citation: F921 — 42 CFR 483.90(i), Safe, Functional, Sanitary, Comfortable Environment
skilled-nursing.frequency: 824 of 13,841 facilities nationally (6.0%); 3 of 296 in Massachusetts (1.0%).

skilled-nursing.finding: No effective pest control programme — evidence of flies, ants, roaches or rodents, or a programme that is not maintained or documented.
skilled-nursing.citation: F925 — 42 CFR 483.90(i)(4), Maintains Effective Pest Control Program
skilled-nursing.frequency: 427 of 13,841 facilities nationally (3.1%); 6 of 296 in Massachusetts (2.0%). Often cited at wide scope.

skilled-nursing.finding: Essential equipment not maintained in safe operating condition, including dietary, laundry and resident-care equipment found broken, soiled, or not maintained to the manufacturer's instructions.
skilled-nursing.citation: F908 — 42 CFR 483.90(d)(2), Essential Equipment in Safe Operating Condition
skilled-nursing.frequency: 554 of 13,841 facilities nationally (4.0%); 9 of 296 in Massachusetts (3.0%).

skilled-nursing.finding: Bloodborne pathogens programme deficiencies — exposure control plan, regulated waste and contaminated laundry handling, decontamination of work surfaces and equipment, sharps containers, PPE and training.
skilled-nursing.frequency: Federal OSHA, October 2024 to September 2025: 43 citations across 10 inspections of skilled nursing facilities — the single most-cited standard for the sector.

assisted-living-cleaning.finding: Staff cannot state the wet contact time for the disinfectant in use, so surfaces are wiped and allowed to dry short of the label time and the product's kill claim is never achieved.
assisted-living-cleaning.citation: CDC Infection Control Assessment and Response (ICAR), environmental cleaning domain
assisted-living-cleaning.frequency: 18.1% of 438 long-term care facilities assessed under a CDC protocol in 2020 were unaware of the contact time for the disinfectants they were using; 206 of those facilities were assisted living.

assisted-living-cleaning.finding: No auditing of hand hygiene and PPE compliance — a written policy exists, but no observational monitoring confirms staff follow it.
assisted-living-cleaning.citation: CDC Infection Control Assessment and Response (ICAR)
assisted-living-cleaning.frequency: 16.8% of the same 438 facilities did no such auditing. Assisted living performed materially worse than nursing homes on several controls in that study.

assisted-living-cleaning.finding: Bloodborne pathogens programme deficiencies — exposure control plan, contaminated laundry, regulated waste, surface decontamination and PPE.
assisted-living-cleaning.frequency: Federal OSHA, October 2024 to September 2025: 9 citations across 3 inspections of assisted living facilities, carrying $37,006 in penalties — the largest penalty total of any standard for the sector.

assisted-living-cleaning.finding: Failure to meet the State Sanitary Code. This is an express condition of certification for every Massachusetts assisted living residence, and a sponsor must forward any Sanitary Code, Building Code or fire safety citation it receives to the state within seven days.

No adequate plan to prevent and limit the spread of communicable disease. Every Massachusetts residence must have one, conforming to accepted principles of universal precautions based on DPH guidance.
Common-area housekeeping is inspected directly by the state. Massachusetts compliance reviews happen at least every two years and must include a physical inspection of the residence's common areas.
assisted-living-cleaning.citation: 651 CMR 12.09 — Compliance Reviews of Assisted Living Residences

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Standard precautions not followed — hand hygiene missed after glove removal, PPE misused, and reusable PPE not reprocessed to the manufacturer's instructions because staff were never trained to clean it.
urgent-care-cleaning.citation: Joint Commission IC.02.01.01 EP 2, Ambulatory Health Care programme
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: Ranked first among the Joint Commission's top five infection control observations for the Ambulatory Health Care programme in 2023. The Joint Commission publishes the ranking, not a percentage.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Low- and intermediate-level disinfection performed incorrectly — wrong product for the surface or device, and label contact time not observed. This is the finding that most directly implicates routine exam-room cleaning.
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: Ranked third among the top five infection control observations for the Ambulatory Health Care programme in 2023.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: High-level disinfection and sterilisation of reusable instruments not performed to the manufacturer's instructions — conflicting instructions never clarified, one cycle used for devices needing different parameters, staff never assessed for competency.
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: First on the Joint Commission's most frequently cited higher-risk requirements for 2023, across the hospital, critical access, ambulatory and office-based surgery programmes.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Clean and sterile supplies stored improperly — under sinks, exposed to splash, on the floor, or in a way that compromises the sterile barrier.
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: Ranked fifth among the top five infection control observations for the Ambulatory Health Care programme in 2023.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Interior spaces not safe and suitable for the care provided — damaged, worn or porous surfaces, flooring and casework that can no longer be effectively cleaned.
urgent-care-cleaning.citation: Joint Commission EC.02.06.01 EP 1 (becomes PE.01.01.01 EP 1–2 in January 2026)
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: A top five Environment of Care opportunity in Joint Commission surveys, data covering May 2024 to May 2025.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Environment not clean and free of odours.
urgent-care-cleaning.citation: Joint Commission EC.02.06.01 EP 20 (becomes PE.01.01.01 EP 3 in January 2026)
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: Listed among the top five Environment of Care opportunities, data covering May 2024 to May 2025.

urgent-care-cleaning.finding: Hazardous chemical risks not minimised across selection, handling, storage and disposal — in practice the unattended housekeeping cart, unlabelled secondary containers of disinfectant, and unsecured chemical storage.
urgent-care-cleaning.citation: Joint Commission EC.02.02.01 EP 5 (becomes PE.02.01.01 EP 4 in January 2026)
urgent-care-cleaning.frequency: A top five Environment of Care opportunity in both hospital and critical access hospital surveys, May 2024 to May 2025.

dental-office-cleaning.finding: No written policies and procedures for routine cleaning and disinfection of environmental surfaces as part of the practice's infection prevention plan.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Environmental Infection Prevention and Control

dental-office-cleaning.finding: Surface barriers on hard-to-clean clinical contact surfaces — chair switches, light handles, computer equipment — not changed between patients, or the surface underneath never checked for soiling once the barrier comes off.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Environmental Infection Prevention and Control

dental-office-cleaning.finding: Non-barrier-protected clinical contact surfaces not cleaned and disinfected with an EPA-registered hospital disinfectant after each patient.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016)

dental-office-cleaning.finding: A low-level disinfectant used where blood contamination is visible. Blood-contaminated clinical contact surfaces require an EPA-registered intermediate-level disinfectant with a tuberculocidal claim.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Best Practices for Environmental Infection Prevention and Control, Dental — clinical contact surfaces

dental-office-cleaning.finding: Manufacturer instructions for the disinfectant not followed — wrong dilution, wrong contact time, expired or badly stored solution, or solutions not made up fresh daily where the label requires it.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016) — amount, dilution, contact time, safe use and disposal

dental-office-cleaning.finding: Regulated medical waste not segregated or contained correctly — blood-soaked gauze and extracted teeth in ordinary trash rather than a leak-resistant biohazard bag, sharps outside a labelled puncture-resistant container.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Best Practices, disposing of medical waste; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4)(iii)

dental-office-cleaning.finding: Dental treatment water not maintained to drinking-water quality of 500 CFU/mL or fewer, or waterline treatment and monitoring not performed to the manufacturer's schedule. Independent water bottles alone are not sufficient.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Dental Unit Water Quality

dental-office-cleaning.finding: No written schedule for cleaning and method of decontamination determined and implemented for the practice.
dental-office-cleaning.citation: 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4)(i); Massachusetts adopts this at 234 CMR 5.05(2), and 234 CMR 5.05(1) requires compliance with the CDC dental infection control guidelines
dental-office-cleaning.frequency: Federal OSHA cited 29 CFR 1910.1030 twelve times across seven inspections of dental offices between October 2024 and September 2025 — the most-cited standard for the sector.

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: No written policies and procedures for routine cleaning and disinfection of the gym, treatment tables and shared equipment, including who is responsible for what.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section I Environmental Cleaning item A

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Cleaning staff have not received job-specific training and competency validation at hire and whenever procedures or products change.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section I item B

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Cleaning is never monitored or assessed — no evidence the clinic checks that it is consistently and correctly performed.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section I item D

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Treatment tables, mats, weights and bands that contact intact skin are wiped but not cleaned and then disinfected between patients at the level the degree of soiling requires.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, non-critical item definition; CDC Core Infection Prevention and Control Practices 5f

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Shared equipment reprocessed in a way the manufacturer never specified. CDC's position is that where no cleaning and disinfection instructions exist, the item is not suitable for multi-patient use.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section II item A; CDC Core Practices 5f

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: High-touch surfaces close to the patient — plinth rails, parallel bars, equipment grips — not cleaned and disinfected on a more frequent schedule than other surfaces.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Core Infection Prevention and Control Practices 5b; CDC Infection Prevention Checklist, Section II item A

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Disinfectant wipes used with insufficient contact time — the surface dries before the label kill time, or the product is past shelf life or badly stored.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section II item B; CDC Core Practices 5b

rehabilitation-clinics.finding: Clean and soiled equipment stored together, with no physical separation to prevent cross-contamination.
rehabilitation-clinics.citation: CDC Core Infection Prevention and Control Practices 5f

medical-office-cleaning.finding: No written schedule for cleaning and method of decontamination based on location, surface type, soil type and the procedures performed. This is an explicit OSHA requirement, not a recommendation.
medical-office-cleaning.frequency: Federal OSHA cited 29 CFR 1910.1030 thirty-three times across twelve inspections of physicians' offices between October 2024 and September 2025 — the most-cited standard for the sector, out of 55 citations in total.

medical-office-cleaning.finding: Contaminated work surfaces not decontaminated after procedures, immediately after a spill, and at the end of a shift where the surface may have become contaminated.
Protective coverings on equipment and surfaces not removed and replaced when contaminated or at the end of the shift.
Reusable bins, pails and cans likely to become contaminated are not inspected and decontaminated on a schedule, and not cleaned immediately when visibly contaminated.
Broken contaminated glassware picked up by hand instead of by mechanical means such as a brush and dustpan, tongs or forceps.
Environmental surfaces in exam rooms, especially those near the patient and frequently touched, not cleaned and then disinfected with an EPA-registered disinfectant.
medical-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section II item A; CDC Core Practices 5b

medical-office-cleaning.finding: Staff cleaning the practice have not been trained on, or issued, the PPE needed to avoid exposure to infectious agents and to the disinfectant chemicals themselves.
medical-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section I item C

medical-office-cleaning.finding: Filled sharps containers not disposed of under state regulated medical waste rules, and regulated waste containers not closable, leak-proof and labelled or colour-coded.
medical-office-cleaning.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist, Section II items I and J; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4)(iii)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: Operating rooms not cleaned and disinfected with an EPA-registered disinfectant after each surgical or invasive procedure. On the CMS surveyor worksheet a 'no' here must be cited as a deficient practice.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CMS Exhibit 351, ASC Infection Control Surveyor Worksheet, Section IV item A — cited against 42 CFR 416.51(a)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: Operating rooms not terminally cleaned daily.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CMS Exhibit 351, Section IV item B — cited against 42 CFR 416.51(a)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: Environmental surfaces in patient care areas not cleaned and disinfected on a regular basis, when spills occur, and when visibly contaminated.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CMS Exhibit 351, Section IV item C — cited against 42 CFR 416.51(a)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: No procedure in place to decontaminate gross spills of blood.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CMS Exhibit 351, Section IV item D — cited against 42 CFR 416.51(a)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: Cleaning staff not included in the centre's documented infection control training. The surveyor worksheet names cleaning staff as a category and asks for documentation proving they were trained.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CMS Exhibit 351, questions 19, 19b, 19d and 19e — related to 42 CFR 416.51(b)

ambulatory-outpatient.finding: The centre cannot document that it considered, selected and implemented nationally recognised infection control guidelines covering environmental services. The infection control programme is a Condition for Coverage, so this can draw a condition-level citation.
Failure to provide a functional and sanitary environment for surgical services — the umbrella finding every environmental cleaning deficiency in a surgery centre is cited under.
Reprocessing area without a one-directional workflow from contaminated to clean and sterile areas — no clear separation between soiled and clean workspaces.
ambulatory-outpatient.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section II item C

specialty-clinics.finding: Diagnostic and therapeutic equipment not disinfected after each use in accordance with recognised standards of practice. In Massachusetts this is a clinic licensure requirement, not guidance.
Cleaning compounds not clearly labelled or not stored in a locked utility closet, and no service sink with hot and cold water for emptying and cleaning housekeeping equipment.
No separation of clean supply storage from the soiled workroom, or a soiled workroom lacking the required work counter, clinical service sink and handwashing sink.
specialty-clinics.citation: 105 CMR 140.204 — Clean Storage and Soiled Holding Areas

specialty-clinics.finding: Hazardous and infectious waste not handled and disposed of properly by the clinic.
Semi-critical instruments and probes — those contacting mucous membranes or non-intact skin — given only a surface wipe rather than at least high-level disinfection, and not pre-cleaned first.
specialty-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, semi-critical item definition and High-Level Disinfection items A to C

specialty-clinics.finding: Shared non-critical devices used across a procedure day — blood pressure cuffs, chin rests, headrests, positioning equipment — not cleaned and disinfected between patients according to the degree of contamination.
specialty-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist, non-critical item definition; CDC Core Practices 5f

specialty-clinics.finding: Frequently touched surfaces in the procedure room not cleaned and then disinfected, and products not used to the manufacturer's dilution, storage, shelf-life and contact time.
specialty-clinics.citation: CDC Infection Prevention Checklist for Outpatient Settings, Section II items A and B

specialty-clinics.finding: No written schedule for cleaning and decontamination, and contaminated work surfaces not decontaminated after procedures, after spills, and at end of shift.
specialty-clinics.frequency: Federal OSHA cited 29 CFR 1910.1030 three times in one inspection of other health practitioners' offices between October 2024 and September 2025 — the most-cited standard for that sector.
